title: Welly Boot Rack
summary: A two-row dowel rack that holds rubber boots upside down by their shafts — bored holes, glue, and screws, no shelf or floor tray involved.
description:
This rack solves boot storage a genuinely different way than a shelf or tray: dowels bored into a 2x3 frame hold each boot upside down by its shaft, which drains and airs them out rather than just corralling them on a floor mat. Mark the dowel locations on the two long 2x3 frame boards first, then drill pilot holes all the way through — this pilot hole is what a screw will later pass through from below to lock the dowel in place, so its size should roughly match your screw shaft.

**Taping the spade bit 3/4" up from its tip is the trick that keeps this simple** — it turns an ordinary 7/8" spade bit into a depth-limited bit without buying a specialty tool, letting you drill a clean 3/4"-deep dowel socket over each pilot hole rather than punching all the way through the frame board.

Glue goes in each hole before the dowel, and the screw through the pilot hole locks it in from underneath. **Keep the longer dowels on the back row and the shorter ones on the front** — this staggered depth is what lets two full rows of boots hang without the front row's boots blocking the back row's.

tools.note: 7/8" spade bit and pilot hole bit.

materials.name: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

steps.title: Cut the frame and the dowels
steps.body: Cut two frame long sides 30" and two frame short ends 14" from the single 2x3 — all four come out of the one 8 ft board with room to spare. From the 7/8" dowels cut six back-row dowels 18" long and six front-row dowels 12" long. Two 18" pieces come out of each 4 ft stick, so three sticks cover the back row and the fourth yields three 12" fronts; the last three fronts have to come off the back-row offcuts, and saw kerf leaves those a hair under 12" — check them before you commit, and shorten all six fronts to match if you want them even. Stand the two 30" boards side by side and confirm they are the same length before drilling: they carry both rows, and a difference here shows up as a rack that won't sit flat.

steps.title: Mark and drill the pilot holes
steps.body: Lay the two 30" frame boards side by side with their ends flush and mark six dowel centers along each, working from the same end of both boards so the two rows line up with each other. Six evenly spaced centers on a 30" board lands them roughly 5" apart, with the first about 2-1/2" in from each end. Drill a pilot hole straight through the board's 1-1/2" thickness at every mark, sized to your screw's shaft rather than its threads — this hole is what a 2-1/2" screw later passes through from below to lock each dowel in.

steps.title: Bore the dowel sockets
steps.body: Wrap tape around the 7/8" spade bit 3/4" up from its tip — that tape is the depth stop, and it turns an ordinary bit into a depth-limited one without buying a specialty tool. Center the bit's spur in each pilot hole and bore until the tape reaches the surface, giving a 3/4"-deep socket with 3/4" of solid wood left beneath it for the screw. Back the bit out and blow the chips clear of every socket; a dowel bottomed on shavings stands proud of its neighbors and the row won't look even.

steps.title: Glue and screw in the dowels
steps.body: Put glue in each socket and press a dowel in until it bottoms out, keeping it square to the board face — a leaning dowel drops the boot off it. Drive a 2-1/2" screw up through each pilot hole from the underside into the end of the dowel to lock it while the glue cures. Put all six 18" dowels in one board and all six 12" dowels in the other: the long row goes at the back, and that staggered depth is what stops the front row of boots from blocking the back row.

steps.title: Assemble the frame
steps.body: Stand the two dowel boards parallel with the dowels pointing up, and close the rectangle with the two 14" end pieces. Drive two 2-1/2" screws through each corner, keeping the ends flush with the tops and the outside faces of the long boards. That is eight screws here plus the twelve already in the dowels — the twenty the material list supplies, with none spare. Run a tape across both diagonals: equal diagonals means the frame is square and will sit flat on the floor.

steps.title: Sand and finish
steps.body: Sand the frame and ease the top edge of every dowel so a wet boot slides on and off without scuffing. Prime and paint, or stain and seal with poly. Let the finish cure fully before hanging boots — a finish that is still soft will stick to rubber and pull off in patches.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

images.alt: Two-row dowel rack for hanging rubber boots upside down
